categoryDescription: Looks at the slow-leak factors that quietly decide whether long-term returns reach the investor or get eaten up by costs: fees, trading drag, tax drag, and operational stability of the issuer and management team. Fees are judged in context — a passive index fund must be cheap; an active, thematic, leveraged, or derivative-income fund must earn its higher fee with documented value-add. Each group uses 4 factors from this file: `expense_ratio`, `management_quality_and_track_record`, and `tax_efficiency` apply to every group; `portfolio_turnover_vs_index` applies where reported turnover is decision-useful; `leverage_cost_drag` applies only to leveraged / inverse products; `commodity_wrapper_structure` applies only to the commodities & digital assets group. Each `factorDescription` carries the generic measurement principle and Pass/Fail rule; `groupInstructions` carries the group-specific perspective (right competitor set, expected band, structural caveats). When the two seem to conflict, follow `groupInstructions`.

factors.factorDescription: Compares the fund's expense ratio against the right competitor set for its structure — not against a universal bar. For passive index funds, compare to the cheapest passive sibling tracking the same exposure (paying 0.50% for an S&P 500 tracker when VOO is 0.03% makes no sense). For active, thematic, derivative-income, leveraged, or tactical-allocation funds, the higher fee is not automatically a Fail — but it must be earned by documented outperformance, downside protection, sustainable income, or a structural feature retail can't get elsewhere; otherwise a cheap passive alternative wins. Fees compound — a 0.50% gap over 30 years quietly erases ~15% of an ending balance. Pass when the fee is at or below the category median for the fund's structure AND, if higher than the cheapest passive sibling, the fund has demonstrated net-of-fee value-add over a multi-year window. Fail when the fee is materially above peer median (more than ~10%) without offsetting value, OR when a passive fund trails its benchmark by meaningfully more than its expense ratio.
factors.factorMetrics: expenseRatio, overviewAdjExpenseRatio, overviewProspectusNetExpenseRatio, overviewCategory, return3y, return5y, returnsTrailing

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Strictest fee bar — passive broad-equity is under 0.10% as standard (VOO 0.03%, VTI 0.03%, IVV 0.03%, SPY 0.09%). Anything above 0.20% on a plain index fund needs a specific reason (active management, factor tilt, ESG screen). Always compare to the cheapest passive sibling on the same exposure (e.g. SPY → VOO / IVV; QQQ → QQQM at 0.15% vs QQQ at 0.20%).
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: 0.10-0.50% is normal (XLK 0.09%, VGT 0.10%, SOXX 0.35%, ARKK 0.75%, niche thematics often 0.50-0.75%). Compare to the broadest passive sector ETF in the same space (XL- series for S&P sectors). For thematic funds, the fee must be earned by outperformance vs a generic sector ETF — if a 0.75% AI fund just tracks the S&P 500 tech sector net of fees, retail is better off with XLK at 0.09%.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: 0.80-1.15% is typical (TQQQ 0.84%, SOXL 0.75%, UPRO 0.91%, SQQQ 0.95%). The sticker fee understates the all-in cost — see `leverage_cost_drag` for the full stack. Pass when the headline fee is in line with peers in the same leverage bucket; Fail when above peer norm without offsetting daily-tracking quality.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Wide range: 0.10-0.85% for commodity wrappers (GLDM 0.10%, IAU 0.25%, GLD 0.40%, SLV 0.50%, USO 0.60%, DBC 0.85%); 0.20-1.50% for spot crypto (IBIT 0.25%, FBTC 0.25%, GBTC 1.50%). Compare physical-backed funds within the same metal (IAU is cheaper than GLD for the same gold exposure). For futures-based commodity funds, note that the headline fee excludes roll cost — that structural drag is covered in `commodity_wrapper_structure`.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: 0.30-0.95% typical (JEPI 0.35%, JEPQ 0.35%, QYLD 0.61%, SPYI 0.68%). The fee must be earned by capped-upside / downside-protection / sustainable yield — compare net total return to a low-cost dividend-equity ETF plus a covered-call overlay. If a 0.65% fund underperforms a 0.10% high-dividend ETF on total return, the fee is not justified.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: 0.10-0.50% is normal (Vanguard target-date 0.08-0.18%, iShares LifePath 0.11-0.27%; active target-date series 0.50%+). All-in-one passive allocation is strong value under 0.20%. Active 0.50%+ needs documented tactical or glide-path advantage over a static DIY blend (e.g. `80% VTI + 20% BND` at near-zero fee).
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Strict bar — passive IG bond / Treasury at 0.03-0.10% (AGG 0.03%, BND 0.03%, VGIT 0.04%, IEF 0.15%) is the benchmark. National muni 0.05-0.40% (MUB 0.05%, VTEB 0.05%). Single-state muni up to 0.50%. TIPS funds 0.04-0.20%. Anything materially above peer median needs a clear reason.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: 0.08-0.65% range — HY corporate (USHY 0.08%, HYG 0.49%, JNK 0.40%), bank loan (BKLN 0.65%), preferred (PFF 0.46%), EM debt (EMB 0.39%, VWOB 0.20%), convertibles (CWB 0.40%). Active credit funds need documented manager alpha net of fees — otherwise USHY at 0.08% or AGG at 0.03% is the honest alternative.

factors.factorDescription: Compares the fund's reported portfolio turnover (percentage of holdings bought and sold each year) to the natural reconstitution turnover of the fund's benchmark index. For passive trackers the fund's turnover should sit close to the index's reconstitution rate — anything materially higher signals excess trading that creates hidden transaction costs and taxable-account drag. For active funds, judge turnover on its own merit against the active-peer category median. Some categories have structurally high turnover (short-duration government, money market, derivative-income with weekly option rolls) — that's the strategy, not undisciplined trading. Pass when turnover is at or near the index's natural rate for passive trackers, OR within the category band for active funds. Fail when turnover is well above the index's reconstitution rate or category peers without a mandate explanation.

groupInstructions.broad-equity: S&P 500 reconstitution turnover runs ~3-5% annually; full Russell reconstitution year hits ~25% on the rebalance month. Passive trackers like VOO / VTI / IVV report 2-5% turnover. Anything above 10% for a plain index tracker signals excess trading without a mandate reason. Style-tilted funds (value / growth / dividend) can run higher (15-40%) because the tilt screen reshuffles holdings.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: S&P sector indices have ~5-15% reconstitution turnover (sector reclassifications, periodic rebalancing). Thematic indices can run 20-60% as theme criteria evolves (ARK funds have run 60-90% historically). Tight passive trackers like XLK / XLV often sit under 10%. Compare to the index's published reconstitution rate where you can find it.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Reported turnover reflects option-roll cadence plus rebalancing of the underlying. Weekly-options funds (QYLG, XYLG) and ELN-based funds (JEPI ~190%) carry mechanically high reported turnover; monthly-options funds (QYLD) report lower because the holding turnover sits inside the option contract. Judge whether the reported turnover matches the stated option-writing strategy.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Static allocation funds (60/40, target-risk) have low turnover (10-30%) — equity / bond rebalancing only. Target-date glide paths show rising turnover near the dated retirement year as the bond sleeve grows. Tactical-allocation funds can hit 100%+ turnover from active calls; the question is whether the active calls beat a passive 60/40 net of the extra trading.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Bonds continually mature or drop out of duration windows, so structural turnover is normal: IG core 30-80%; intermediate Treasury 40-100%; ultrashort / MMF 100%+ (short maturities mechanically force rolls); muni 15-50%. Compare to the index turnover (Bloomberg US Aggregate runs ~10-15% from rolls and maturities) and to category-typical for the duration bucket.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: HY / bank-loan / EM-debt funds have higher turnover than IG (40-100%) from credit decisions and ratings drift. Bank-loan funds roll constantly as loans pay down. Compare to the matching credit index reconstitution rate and to active-peer category median.

factors.factorTitle: Issuer Quality, Manager Tenure & Track Record
factors.factorDescription: Looks at four things together: (1) issuer reputation and operational scale — Vanguard, iShares (BlackRock), SPDR (State Street), Schwab, Invesco, Fidelity run tight, well-supervised operations; smaller / newer issuers carry operational risk. (2) Manager tenure and continuity — turnover within the past 2-3 years on an active or strategy fund is a yellow flag. (3) Fund age and operational history — 10+ years means multiple market cycles evaluated; 5-10Y is decent signal; 3-5Y partial; under 3Y is effectively 'new' and must lean on issuer credibility and strategy simplicity. (4) Mandate stability — quietly changing benchmark, strategy, or category breaks the usability of the historical record and is a real red flag. For passive broad-index funds, issuer quality dominates over named managers (managers are largely symbolic for pure-replication). For active, allocation, credit, derivative-income, and strategy funds, manager continuity matters as much as issuer. AUM trajectory context is referenced here only as background — the live AUM-trajectory call lives in the past-returns `aum_growth_trend` factor. Pass when the issuer is established AND the fund has 5+ years of stable mandate AND manager continuity is at least 3-5 years, OR the fund is younger than 5Y but comes from an established issuer running a proven strategy. Fail when a niche / newer issuer is running a complex active strategy with thin operational history, OR documented recent strategy / benchmark / category changes, OR active-fund manager churn without a clear succession story. Do NOT Fail on age alone — anchor on issuer credibility and strategy simplicity when track record is short.
factors.factorMetrics: issuer, numberOfManagers, longestTenure, averageTenure, advisors, currentManagers, inceptionDate, strategyText, overviewCategory

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Issuer reputation dominates — for passive index trackers, named managers are largely symbolic. Mega-issuers (Vanguard, BlackRock, State Street, Schwab, Fidelity, Invesco) are the safe choices. Smaller issuers running a passive broad-equity fund are acceptable when AUM and tracking quality are healthy. Don't flag a 33-year manager tenure on a 33-year-old fund as a strength — that's just fund age, not a comparative signal.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: For passive sector / thematic funds (XL- series, VGT, etc.), issuer matters most. For actively-managed thematic ETFs (ARKK, KraneShares, Catalyst), manager continuity is decisive — flag any churn. Theme reclassifications (a 'robotics' fund quietly becoming an 'AI' fund) are real mandate changes.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Issuer operational track record on leveraged products is the anchor — ProShares, Direxion, GraniteShares dominate the space. Smaller issuers running 3x products without a track record carry real operational risk (the inverse-volatility blowups of 2018 / 2020 hit smaller issuers).
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Issuer matters more here than for plain equity because of custody, audit, and structural integrity — major issuers (State Street/SPDR, BlackRock/iShares, Invesco, WisdomTree, Grayscale, Fidelity) carry the operational scale; smaller issuers running spot-commodity or crypto wrappers need scrutiny on custody arrangements and audit cadence.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Manager continuity is decisive for these strategy-driven funds. JPMorgan (JEPI / JEPQ), Global X (QYLD / RYLD / XYLD), NEOS (SPYI / QQQI), Innovator (defined outcome), Amplify, KraneShares have established teams. Newer issuers running complex options strategies with thin track records carry real execution risk.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Glide-path architect AND active-sleeve manager continuity both matter. Vanguard, BlackRock, T. Rowe Price, Fidelity, American Funds are the establishment for target-date series. For tactical / global allocation, individual decision-makers drive outcomes — flag any manager change.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: For passive IG / Treasury funds, issuer dominates. For active IG core / core-plus, manager continuity matters — PIMCO, Vanguard, iShares, Schwab, T. Rowe Price are the establishment. For single-state muni, newer issuers are acceptable when issuer / advisor research depth is documented.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Active credit demands manager expertise — HY / bank-loan / EM-debt funds rely on credit-research teams. PIMCO, BlackRock, Eaton Vance, T. Rowe Price, Nuveen, Lord Abbett are the active-credit establishment. For passive HY / preferred ETFs, issuer scale is sufficient.

factors.factorDescription: Measures how much of the fund's return is lost to tax drag in a taxable brokerage account, and what tax character its distributions have. ETFs are structurally tax-efficient — in-kind creation / redemption keeps capital-gain distributions rare — but not all ETFs are equal. Three dimensions matter: (1) capital-gain distribution history (a flag for active or thematic equity ETFs and for daily-leveraged products); (2) distribution composition — qualified dividends taxed favorably (max 23.8% federal long-term) vs ordinary income (marginal rate up to 37%+) vs short-term gain (marginal) vs return-of-capital (ROC is tax-deferred but lowers cost basis, not a free lunch); (3) structural quirks — K-1 reporting for partnership-structured commodity funds adds tax-time friction; physically-backed precious metals are taxed at the collectibles rate (max 28%); muni-bond income is federal-tax-exempt (and state-tax-exempt for in-state holders); daily-leveraged products generate frequent cap-gain distributions from the swap-reset mechanism. For tax-deferred accounts (IRA, 401(k)) this factor is less decisive — but most retail dollars sit in taxable accounts, so it still matters. Pass when the fund has no meaningful recent cap-gain distribution history (passive equity) OR distribution character is well-disclosed and reasonable for the strategy. Fail when there are material cap-gain distributions despite a passive structure, OR high-yield income is largely ROC / short-term gain without clear disclosure, OR the structure creates unexpected tax burden (K-1) without offsetting benefit.
factors.factorMetrics: overviewTurnover, reportedTurnoverPct, dividendYield, overviewSecYield, overviewTtmYield, overviewCategory, strategyText

groupInstructions.broad-equity: ETF structure makes broad-equity index funds extremely tax-efficient — VOO / VTI / IVV essentially never pay capital-gain distributions. Qualified-dividend share of distributions is high. Active or actively-managed broad-equity ETFs occasionally pay cap-gains; check the recent history. Hold in taxable accounts without concern for the right fund.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Most sector / thematic ETFs are tax-efficient via in-kind redemption. Active thematic funds with high turnover can pay capital-gain distributions; check the recent history. REIT-focused funds pay non-qualified dividends taxed at marginal rates — flag explicitly.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Materially tax-inefficient — daily swap-reset generates frequent capital-gain distributions, often taxed as short-term gains at marginal rates. Best held in a tax-advantaged account, but these are traded short-term anyway so the tax friction hits every realized trade. State this explicitly for retail.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Structure changes everything. (1) Physically-backed precious metals (GLD, IAU, SLV, PPLT) → collectibles rate (max 28% in taxable accounts, higher than long-term cap-gains 15-20%). (2) Futures-based commodity funds (USO, UNG, DBC, DBA) → often K-1 partnership reporting; mark-to-market 60/40 tax on Section 1256 contracts (60% long-term, 40% short-term regardless of holding period). (3) Spot crypto wrappers (IBIT, FBTC, ETHA) → 1099 grantor-trust pass-through; relatively simple tax. Name the structure and its tax implication explicitly.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Distribution composition is the central tax question. JEPI distributions are qualified dividends + ELN income taxed as ordinary income. QYLD-style funds often have a large ROC share (tax-deferred but lowers cost basis). Headline yield alone misleads — quote the ROC share and frame the after-tax effective yield at a typical retail bracket. Best held in IRA / 401(k); flag for retail in taxable accounts.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Generally tax-efficient — qualified dividends + interest-income mix matches the underlying sleeves. The bond-sleeve portion generates ordinary interest income, so allocation funds with heavy bond sleeves (conservative, target-date nearing retirement at 40-60% bonds) are less tax-efficient than pure equity. Better held in tax-advantaged accounts for the bond-heavy end of the glide path.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Treasury and agency interest is federal-only taxable (state-tax-exempt). Muni interest is federal-tax-exempt (and state-tax-exempt for in-state holders) — quote tax-equivalent yield at a representative bracket (~32% federal, plus state for in-state funds) so retail can compare to taxable bond peers. TIPS funds generate phantom income (inflation-adjusted principal taxable annually) — best held in IRA.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: HY / bank-loan / EM-debt / multisector distributions are ordinary interest income, taxed at marginal rates — less tax-efficient than qualified-dividend equity. Best held in IRA / 401(k). Preferred-stock ETFs are a mix of qualified and ordinary distributions — less tax-efficient than common-stock dividends. EM debt may carry foreign withholding taxes.

factors.factorDescription: Leveraged / inverse ETFs carry three stacked costs retail underestimates: (1) the headline expense ratio (typically 0.80-1.15%); (2) embedded financing cost inside the swaps or futures used to achieve daily leverage — often adds another 50-150 bps on top of the expense ratio and sits OUTSIDE the sticker fee (currently roughly the SOFR rate × (leverage - 1) for long-leveraged funds); (3) daily-rebalancing friction in volatile markets (volatility drag), which compounds against any holding beyond a few days. The all-in cost drag on a multi-month hold is frequently 2-3%+ — materially higher than the headline fee, and on a 3x long-equity fund the realistic annual cost stack can hit 6-9% in mixed markets. This sits on top of the path-dependency decay covered in past-returns and the daily-tracking question covered in risk-analysis. Pass when the fund is correctly marketed as a short-term trading vehicle, the headline fee is in line with leveraged-peer norms, bid-ask supports rapid trading, and issuer disclosure on financing costs is transparent. Fail when the headline fee is above peer norm without offsetting tracking quality, OR structural issues (wide bid-ask, poor daily-leverage fidelity, thin volume) magnify the true cost. Retail takeaway: not suitable for multi-week holding regardless of how cheap the sticker fee looks.
factors.factorMetrics: expenseRatio, overviewAdjExpenseRatio, overviewProspectusNetExpenseRatio, overviewTurnover, marketBidAskSpread, return1y, returnsAnnual

groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Quote all three cost layers explicitly as concrete numbers: (a) sticker expense ratio, (b) estimated embedded swap financing cost (roughly SOFR × (leverage - 1) for long-leveraged equity funds — in a 4-5% SOFR environment that adds ~8-10% to a 3x fund's annual cost), (c) observed daily-rebalancing slippage in choppy markets (typically 1-3% per year). The all-in for a 3x long-equity fund is often 6-9%+ per year in mixed markets. Reinforce: usable for days-to-weeks directional bets only, never as a buy-and-hold position.

factors.factorDescription: Looks at HOW the commodity or digital-asset fund actually delivers its exposure — the biggest differentiator in this group. Three wrapper types each have different tracking, cost, custody, and tax profiles: (1) Physical-backed precious metals (GLD, IAU, SLV) — fund holds bars in audited vaults; tracking gap vs spot is typically only the expense ratio; custody quality (vault, insurance, audit cadence) is the main risk. (2) Futures-based commodity funds (USO, UNG, DBC, BCI, DBA) — fund holds short-dated futures and rolls them monthly / quarterly; contango (rolling into higher-priced futures) creates persistent NAV erosion (USO lost ~94% of NAV since inception); backwardation (rolling into cheaper futures) is positive carry. (3) Spot crypto grantor trusts (IBIT, FBTC, GBTC, BITB, ETHA) — fund holds the underlying coin with a qualified custodian; tracking is tight (custody fee only); the custody arrangement (cold storage, qualified custodian, insurance) is the main structural risk. Quote which wrapper type the fund uses, the fund's tracking gap vs the spot underlying over 5Y / 10Y, and any custody / structural concerns. Pass when the wrapper type fits the retail use case (physical for direct metal ownership, futures for short-term directional bet, spot for plain crypto holding) AND the structural cost is in line with peers. Fail when futures-based contango drag has eaten multi-year price returns without an offsetting reason, OR custody / audit / issuer track record raises real concerns.
factors.factorMetrics: expenseRatio, overviewAdjExpenseRatio, return1y, return3y, return5y, return10y, returnsAnnual, change5y, change10y, strategyText

groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Always name the wrapper type explicitly. **Physical-backed precious metals** (GLD, IAU, SLV, SIVR, PPLT) — tracking gap vs spot LBMA / COMEX price should be roughly the expense ratio; flag any larger gap as custody / management drag. **Futures-based commodity wrappers** (USO oil, UNG natural gas, DBC / DBA broad / agricultural, BCI broad-basket) — quote the spot vs fund gap over 5Y / 10Y; USO lost ~94% of NAV since inception from contango; DBC is constructed to minimize roll cost but still has structural drag. **Spot crypto grantor trusts** (IBIT, FBTC, BITB, GBTC for BTC; ETHA, ETHE for ETH) — tracking gap vs CME CF reference rate should be roughly the expense ratio; flag custody structure (Coinbase Custody is the dominant qualified custodian for spot-BTC ETFs in 2024-25; insurance terms matter). **Single-currency wrappers** (UUP USD, FXE EUR, FXY JPY) — these are typically swap-based or hold short-duration money-market instruments in the target currency; tracking gap shows up as combined swap cost + cash interest rate differential. Name the sub-type the fund belongs to and tie the structural cost story directly to retail's intended use case.
